speaker: interviewer
text: --About how you were invited back to your hometown.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you decided to go back and meet up with your family and your brothers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, kind of reluctantly. Accepted and when we were in the airport, ready to board Lufthansa, I saw Germans again for the first time, since I left, a group of them. They were all Aryan looking, blond, and boldly speaking the harsh language. It sounded ugly to my ear. And I, I felt so uncomfortable. I said to Earl, I want to go home. [LAUGHS] This was in the airport. And he, of course, empathized with me. But he, he was so supportive of me doing this. And he thought it would be good for me. But it turned out that this group-- this noisy group-- were young students that were in Wisconsin. They were a, a Stuttgart orchestra that were doing some cultural exchange. But it-- nevertheless, these people are so all the same, homogeneous, that it, it's just so different than this country, where we have this pluralism. And I, I, I learned to love what we have in this country, that everybody contributes to it and doesn't matter what colors. It's a patchwork quilt. And it's just beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, but nevertheless, the trip took place. And the government there, under Horst Hinck, the Oberburgermeister of Leverkusen, they did an outstanding job. I cannot tell you the hospitality, the sensitivity they showed to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: That mayor made many, many speeches, including ones in which he put the yarmulke on his head. The day of the Holocaust-- the actual Holocaust commemoration of Kristallnacht, we went to the Jewish Cemetery together as a group. There were about 33 in our party. There were some of the residents. But there were also some spouses and some children of these former residents of Leverkusen that came back. We went to the Jewish Cemetery. And by the way, we were accompanied by a contingent from Israel. Leverkusen formed a partner city with Nazareth Illit in Israel. And the mayor of that town and two other officials from the town came to keep us company, it was just wonderful. And we had security along with us. They did everything they possibly could. Now, when we entered the cemetery, we went through the iron gate. And we went straight down the middle aisle. And I know exactly where my father's grave was located. The willow tree was still there, but no grave, no grave marker.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I paused there. And I identified that place. And other people did the same thing. But the group gathered at my father's grave. And one of the Israelis tapped on me-- on the shoulder, and he said, can we do the El malei rachamim with you? And I said, yes, please. And it was very tearful and very beautiful that we were able to do this for my father. And my mother would be thrilled if she knew that we were there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Afterwards, Herr, Herr-- Oberburgermeister put on a yarmulke at the graveside. And we also had a mass memorial for all the victims of Leverkusen. They had put up a memorial for them, which was just a rough-hewn stone with a dedication on it. Later on, they got the money to put a more formal stone up, a big one with all the names of the people that perished. There were about 130 Jews from Leverkusen. And 30 of them were killed. Others emigrated. And others just left, and they went to all over the world. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] From there, we went to the place where the former synagogue was in Opladen. There was nothing there. But that day, they'd-- there was a little memorial stone that was put up several years earlier. But this time, it was a formal dedication. They called this the Synagogenplatz. That means the Place of the Synagogue. And a big memorial. And people spoke. One of the residents, it was a religious Jew from-- that lives in England. He made a magnificent speech, to never ever forget what happened there. And the mayor, with his yarmulke, and said [NON-ENGLISH]. The mayor from Nazareth Illit made a beautiful speech in Hebrew and in German. And it was very, very touching. Security was very tight. Because there are skinheads over there. In fact, they marched that night. The next day, we saw in the newspaper what happened with the skinheads, and how the police took care of them. We read other reports, what was going on all over the country. Germany really made a mass effort to memorialize Kristallnacht. In all the book stores, there was literature, all over the place. On the television, there were nuns that said they were dedicating the rest of their lives to the Jewish people because of what happened. There was one little town where they would not let the Nazis-- they were given the word to destroy the synagogue, and the people wouldn't do it. It was boarded up, and it was reopened 50 years later. It was not burned. There were cemeteries that weren't touched. Of course, most of them were. But there are very unusual things that we came across. Also, what the government so sensitively did is, the first night we were there, they allowed us to get reacquainted. My brothers talked to people they shared pads with in Auschwitz. And they hadn't seen them since--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Dachau, excuse me. Yes. That they hadn't seen since then. And they shared experiences, and what happened in their lives. So we were reunited amongst ourselves. The next evening, they took us to a, a residence for senior citizens, in total privacy. No one else in town knew where we were meeting. And they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why? Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they wanted to have total security, no skinheads, no demonstrations, no risk. They wanted to have us feel secure, OK? They had a security man with us at all times. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that bother you that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt-- it made me feel good that they had these precautions. Because-- there were some strange feelings. There were strange feelings all over the place for me. Because they took us for a ride through the town when we arrived. And I said, I know where my house is, because it's across from that church. That church was still there. You could see it for miles. And I said, I've got to go there. I have to go there. And they said, you can't, because we can't take the bus in there. It's a walking zone, you know, it's a mall, like. But the next day, you'll be able to go. And the next day, we went to Breidenbachstrasse. Sign is still there. And I knew where my house was, in relationship to the church across the street. Of course, it looked totally different. And they used the property in back of it to build more. They didn't keep that big yard. And it's a total modern facade now. But then I went to see my school. And my husband looked at me, and he said, I was just so excited, you know. This is where this is, and this is where that is. I hadn't even thought about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I remembered when we took the bus ride, I, I could hear the air raids, the sirens going off in my head. And what's going to happen now? Are we going to get hit? It just all came back to me. And I saw the rooftops, because these are things you looked at when an air raid took place. And would it still be there afterwards? So all those things came flooding back to me. It was important to go and re-experience that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when they took us to the senior citizen complex, we were greeted by all the old neighbors, people that worked for my family and our business, people I went to school with that I last saw when we were children, they were now adults. They came to see us, because they knew we were coming. They did put our names in the newspaper. And they were to contact the government.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had marvelous reunions. And they were so happy to see us. And we shared mutual hardships. So do you think this trip helped give you a sense of closure--

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: --In your feelings that you'd been carrying with you for many years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, yes. I have a new image now. I have a different image. I have the same image, but more, because life there started up again, out of ashes, too. Everybody was destroyed. We lost six million, and so did they. They did. They, they, they told me-- I heard some horror stories there-- about one German that I had no connection with. They had another evening of, of officials that they got us together with. And they had a, a man from the Bundestag in, in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the capital, Bonn, came to talk to us. And he made a very emotional speech. He was a young man. He was appalled at what happened. And his teacher told him how he was against the Nazis. And that was the only time he could say it. The next day, the man was silenced, or he would have died. He lost his freedom of speech. They-- he couldn't say any more.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we-- and this man sent us-- Earl said, he understood some of it. And he said, I'd like to read in English what you said, sir. Would you mind having that translated? And he said, you'll get it. And we did. Did you have a sense of forgiveness when you left, in your heart?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. There was not a sense of forgiveness. It's just life is different today. The same people are not there-- hopefully not. Because if they were, I wouldn't want to see them, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: What message would you like to leave to your children and your family and friends and people trying to learn from your tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, I would tell them the same thing that I heard all my life after leaving Germany. Don't forget what happened. Don't let it happen again. You have to remember. You have to talk about it. Go to temple, identify yourself as a Jew. Enjoy your Judaism. Enjoy this country and the privileges that you have and the rights that you have. They're not free. They were fought for.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ruth, thank you for sharing your story with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. That's my father, Leopold Heumann. The picture was taken in his World War I uniform. I believe it was taken in Leverkusen at some time, I'm not sure exactly when. It came over to this country with our belongings in the crate that was sent ahead of time, before we even got here. That's my mama looking like a Gibson girl. It was around the turn of the century time. This was Mom and Dad's engagement picture in Cologne, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name-- maiden name was Selma Sarah Rosenberg. Those are the two houses that my father built as a pioneer in the town of Wiesdorf, part of Leverkusen. It was called Leverkusen-Wiesdorf. Wiesdorf is the older part of the town, where Bayer aspirin was discovered, and the IG Farben was located. It was a munitions factory. I talked about this earlier. Dad built these two adjacent apartment buildings. We lived on the one on the right. Our store was on the ground level. Later on, it was remodeled with huge windows and a big sign that said, Heumann and Sons Paints. Now, we lived on that first story with the balcony. And it was directly across from the church. You can see the fence of the churchyard. And that's where we lived. It was the family home. These are my four brothers before I was born. The oldest is on the right, and that's Alfred Heumann. Next to him is the youngest, Joseph Heumann. And the one next to him, standing up in the middle there, is Werner Heumann. And the one on the left is Helmut Heumann.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken, I believe, in the town of Leverkusen. Here's a picture of me. I would estimate that it was 6 or 7 years old at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in the backyard where we lived. This is the year 1938. It was taken in Israel, when I attended the wedding of my brother Werner, the bridegroom, and the beautiful bride, Ruth. This is the Jewish school I attended, when I was no longer able to go to the public German school. This is the school in Cologne. These are all Jewish students. And it's a very sad picture. I don't know how many of these kids survived. I'm the one on the top-- in the top row on the left, the third one, with a little headband on. This is my passport. It has a red J, standing for Jew. And it also identifies me further by the name Sara, which was added to my name by the Nazis by law. Sara Ruth Heumann. This is the Nazi area-- era stamp that was affixed to that passport. This is the passport, which allowed me to finally leave the country, when I was 11 years old. This is the guy I fell in love with, my Earl. He's already decorated. And it didn't stop there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken when he was a-- a young soldier.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken during the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken during the war. This is Ruth and Earl Arkiss now. It was the former Ruth Heumann. They were at an engagement party in their honor, in Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1952. This is Leslie, our pride and joy. It was taken when she graduated high school from Rich East High School in Park Forest, Illinois.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken at the high school. This is at Rich East High School, when Gary Arkiss graduated. And he is our one and only wonderful son.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this taken? '76?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. 19-- This beautiful lady was smiling so profusely, because she-- it was her 80th birthday. And she had all her children together. This is our Omar Selma, the grandmother to my children and my beloved mother, Selma Heumann, on her 80th birthday. She died about a week later. Here we are at a party five years ago, approximately. Our son-in-law, John Kumler, on the left, with his wife, our daughter Leslie. In the middle is our son, Gary Arkiss. There is Ruth, myself, and my beloved husband, Earl.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken at a party in Chicago.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1991. Well, this is my beloved. And I don't think I would have made it without him. He's just been my source of strength. And he has encouraged me. His life experiences were very different than mine. But fortunately, we have each other. And we complement one another.

speaker: interviewee
text: She has enriched my life immeasurably. Without her, I can only exist. With her, I live. She has contributed to me and brought to me insights that I myself lacked. And going back to our trip to Germany brought home to us, with such force and insight, the necessity to be proud of our Judaism and to have that Judaism as a continuum for succeeding generations, not only for our children, but for all Jews. Because all Jews represent-- to me, as far as I'm concerned-- my immortality. The Jewish people are a family. And being a family, what happens to any Jew, happens to me. This is what we believe in. This is what we will pursue. And this is what we accept, as our basic social philosophy.

speaker: interviewee
text: So far, it has only been 44 years that we're together.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it has been 44 years of real bliss. It has been 44 years of, of the aspect of life and living. And often I say to myself, where did the years go, because they were so happy. And we intend to continue that degree of happiness. More than loving my wife, I like her. She's a wonderful personality and a person of tremendous compassion.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll keep him, too. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: That was great. Thank you so much for testifying for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.
